column2: A desktop shows a black screen before the operating system loads and emits a repeating beep pattern. What should a technician do with that beep pattern?
column3: Record the exact pattern and check the documentation for that system or BIOS.
column4:
Why

POST beep and code meanings vary by firmware and manufacturer. In an interview, the strong answer is to capture the evidence and use the correct reference, not to guess from a generic code list.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield POST Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC powers on, never reaches the OS, and shows no video. POST beeps are present. Which troubleshooting direction is most appropriate?
column3: Treat it as a pre-boot hardware or firmware issue, then use the beep pattern and documentation to narrow video, memory, CPU, or BIOS configuration causes.
column4:
Reasoning

The symptom happens before the operating system loads, so starting with Windows repair would be premature. POST evidence helps separate hardware and firmware theories from OS theories.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic POST Boot Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says their PC tries to boot from a USB installer they left plugged in. What is the best first fix?
column3: Remove the bootable media and confirm the BIOS or UEFI boot order if the problem returns.
column4:
Help Desk Logic

Start with the simple, reversible cause. If removable media caused the symptom once, boot order should be checked only if the system keeps selecting the wrong startup device.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.48; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Boot Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop loses the correct date, time, and firmware settings every time it is unplugged. What is the most likely hardware cause?
column4:
Symptom Pattern

The motherboard battery maintains firmware settings while the system is powered off. Replacing it is more targeted than reinstalling the OS or replacing storage.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic CMOS Battery Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation begins blue-screening during startup immediately after a new device driver was installed. What is the best next step before replacing hardware?
column3: Boot into Safe Mode or another recovery path and roll back the driver or use System Restore.
column4:
Troubleshooting Logic

The timing points to the recent driver change. A least-disruptive rollback tests that theory before opening the case or replacing parts.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield BSOD Drivers Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user reports, "The program gave me an error and closed," but they do not know what the error said. What should the technician ask for first?
column3: Ask for the exact error text or a screenshot, plus what the user was doing when it appeared.
column4:
Interview Angle

Good support starts by improving the quality of the evidence. Exact text, screenshots, and reproduction context are more useful than a vague ticket note.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic CrashScreens Documentation Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says the computer is running but the monitor is blank. What should you check before swapping hardware?
column3: Check monitor power, the signal cable, the selected input, and brightness.
column4:
Why

These checks are fast, noninvasive, and common causes of a blank display. Swapping the monitor is reasonable only after the basic external checks are verified.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Display Cables Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop has no lights, no fans, and no response when the power button is pressed. What is the first area to verify?
column3: Verify the power source path: outlet, power strip, power cable, and power supply input.
column4:
Reasoning

No signs of life can be caused outside the computer. Checking the power path first avoids replacing internal components when the system is not receiving power.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A PC's case fans spin, but drives and the motherboard do not appear to power up and the system never POSTs. What should you avoid assuming?
column3: Do not assume the power supply is fully healthy just because fans spin. Check power connections and power supply output.
column4:
Exam Trap

Fans can require less power than other devices. Spinning fans are evidence that some power exists, not proof that every required output is correct.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Power POST Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says their computer is "slow today." What tool should you check first to narrow whether CPU, memory, or disk activity is high?
column4:
Why

Task Manager gives quick evidence about resource use. That evidence is better than guessing between malware, disk pressure, updates, a bad application, or hardware failure.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A laptop performs normally on AC power but becomes sluggish when running on battery. What setting should you check before replacing hardware?
column3: Check for a power-saving mode or policy that throttles performance on battery.
column4:
Support Logic

The symptom changes with the power state. That points toward power management before CPU, memory, or storage replacement.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Performance Laptop Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A gaming PC shuts down after several minutes of heavy graphics load but works at the desktop. What should you check first?
column3: Check cooling: fans, airflow, heat sinks, dust blockage, and temperature readings.
column4:
Reasoning

Load-dependent shutdowns often point toward heat. Confirm the temperature and cooling theory before replacing the graphics card or power supply.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Overheating Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user reports smoke and a burning smell from a desktop. What is the safest first action?
column3: Disconnect power and stop using the system until the damaged component is found and replaced.
column4:
Safety

Burning odor is not a normal troubleshooting state. Do not keep power applied to "see if it still works."
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Safety Power Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation randomly powers off with no warning. The user says it happens more often during video rendering. Which evidence should you gather next?
column3: Check Event Viewer, fan status, heat sinks, and temperature readings during or after load.
column4:
Narrowing

The load pattern makes heat a strong theory, but logs and diagnostics still matter. A good technician gathers evidence before replacing parts.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Shutdowns Overheating Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Only one application closes unexpectedly; the rest of Windows remains stable. What should you check before reinstalling the application?
column3: Check the application's error message, Event Viewer, and Reliability Monitor.
column4:
Why

Application crashes are narrower than system crashes. Logs and Reliability Monitor can show patterns and error details that guide the next step.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Applications Logs Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A desktop makes a clicking sound that changes speed when internal fan speed changes. What component should you inspect first?
column3: Inspect the fans for obstruction, bearing problems, or contact with a cable.
column4:
Reasoning

Noise type and timing matter. A clicking sound tied to fan speed is more useful evidence than a broad "replace the computer" conclusion.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.50; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic Noises Fans Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: You cannot resolve an intermittent blue-screen issue and must escalate it. What information should you include in the ticket?
column3: Include symptoms, timing, recent changes, exact error text or screenshots, Event Viewer details, diagnostics run, and results of each step.
column4:
Professional Practice

Escalation is not just "send it up." Good notes let the next technician continue the investigation without repeating all discovery work.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.49; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.1
column9: A+::220-1201::5.1 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingHardware Basic HighYield Escalation Documentation Interview Source::Messer-v170
